ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for jackfruit fritter; mapped to the broader fritter food product class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fritters and doughnuts) used as the closest defensible category; no jackfruit-specific fritter code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for jackfruit fritter; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of jackfruit fritter is one piece weighing approximately 50 g, commonly consumed as a snack or breakfast item at hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Jackfruit Fritter — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Jackfruit Fritter (CR ID F11090022). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical Singapore source at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Deep-Fried Battered Snacks — Analogous Estimate Based on Banana Fritter (GI ~50) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for jackfruit fritter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 is estimated by analogy to banana fritter and similar deep-fried battered fruit snacks (GI range 45–55) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 24.3 × 0.50) / 100 = 6.1 for a 50 g serving (12.15 g carbohydrate). Input record GI value of 50 and GL of 12.2 per 100 g are consistent with this analogous estimate and are retained.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (172 mg/100 g) combined with high fat from deep-frying and elevated sugar content
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit jackfruit fritter to occasional small portions, as the deep-frying process adds significant saturated fat and the sodium content of 172 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium load when multiple pieces are consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (10.4 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate batter with moderate GI (50) leading to meaningful postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit jackfruit fritter, as the combination of 24.3 g carbohydrate and 10.4 g sugar per 100 g — delivered via a deep-fried refined flour batter — produces a significant glycaemic load that can elevate postprandial blood glucose; raw jackfruit in small portions is a lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds saturated fat and likely trans fat, raising LDL cholesterol risk; high sugar content also contributes to dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid jackfruit fritter, as deep-frying in oil contributes saturated fat (estimated 1.8 g/100 g) and potential trans fats from repeated oil heating, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk; the high sugar content further promotes triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and likely trans fats, both of which are pro-inflammatory hard gates for rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid jackfruit fritter entirely, as the deep-frying preparation generates AGEs and likely trans fats that promote systemic inflammation and can exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jackfruit fritter is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian snack made by coating ripe jackfruit pieces in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-frying them until golden and crispy. The result is a warm, crispy-on-the-outside, soft-and-sweet-on-the-inside snack with the distinctive tropical sweetness of jackfruit complemented by the savoury, oily crunch of the fried batter. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, pasar malam (night markets), and roadside stalls across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia, typically consumed as a breakfast item or afternoon snack. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 201 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and 24.3 g carbohydrate with 10.4 g sugar and 3.2 g dietary fibre. The sodium content is 172 mg/100 g. The deep-frying process significantly increases the fat content compared to raw jackfruit and introduces saturated fat and potential trans fats from heated cooking oil. Clinically, this food is of high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar and refined carbohydrate content, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to the fat profile from deep-frying. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low-moderate), but the glycaemic load per 50 g serving is approximately 6.1, making portion control essential for metabolically vulnerable individuals. The food is plant-based, halal, and dairy-free, but contains gluten from wheat flour.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a jackfruit fritter?
Is jackfruit fritter suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11090022) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable deep-fried battered fruit snacks. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried battered snacks as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable deep-fried battered snacks (e.g., banana fritter analogues), as the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown for this specific item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional deep-fried preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific food item as of 2026. 3. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on banana fritter and similar deep-fried battered fruit snacks from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for jackfruit fritter has been published. 4. The input record listed dietary_tags including 'wholegrain' — this tag has been removed as the batter is made from refined wheat flour, not wholegrain flour. 5. The input health_suitability entry for hypertension contained an internal contradiction in its reasoning; the revised health_context entry reflects a corrected clinical assessment. 6. Calcium (77 mg/100 g) and iron (0.5 mg/100 g) are sourced from HPB and retained as reported.
